levels.name: The Golden River
levels.historical_fact: TUTORIAL: The Rioni, known in antiquity as the Phasis, carried mountain gold toward lowland settlements. Defend the river villages where later Colchian legends took root.

levels.name: Tribes of the Rioni
levels.historical_fact: TUTORIAL: Before centralized rule, clan communities guarded crossings, forests, and trade paths. These early defenses shaped the later Colchian world.

levels.name: Medea's Grove
levels.historical_fact: TUTORIAL: Medea belongs to mythic tradition, yet sacred groves and ritual spaces were central to early communities. Master upgrades and timing under shrine protection.

levels.name: Shores of Pontus
levels.historical_fact: Greek sailors reached the eastern Black Sea seeking timber, metals, and exchange. Coastal contact brought wealth and danger in equal measure.

levels.name: Dragon of the Fleece
levels.historical_fact: Myth remembers a dragon guarding the Golden Fleece. This battle is myth-framed and marks the end of the earliest chapter.

levels.name: Fleece of the Mountain Stream
levels.historical_fact: Traditions of fleece-based gold collection likely echo real stream mining practices. Hold the mountain channels where wealth was gathered.

levels.name: Forest Pass of Colchis
levels.historical_fact: Forest passes linked upland and river communities long before formal borders. Control these chokepoints to protect the heartland.

levels.name: Colchian Forge Village
levels.historical_fact: By the early first millennium BC, bronze and early iron craft supported stronger tools and defenses. Protect the forge settlements.

levels.name: Watchfires of the Caucasus
levels.historical_fact: Watchfires and ridge beacons coordinated warnings across difficult terrain. Preserve the highland line.

levels.name: Devi Chief's Ravine
levels.historical_fact: In Georgian folklore, Devi chiefs command brute force and fear. Defeat this mythic threat at the chapter frontier.

levels.name: Phasis Trade Road
levels.historical_fact: Phasis became a major exchange corridor in the classical period. Defend caravans that carry timber, metal, and grain.

levels.name: Sacred Oak Clearing
levels.historical_fact: Pre-Christian ritual groves and oak sanctuaries held social and spiritual weight. Hold the clearing to keep the valley united.

levels.name: Black Sea Cliffs
levels.historical_fact: Black Sea cliffs and inlets offered both shelter and exposure. Repel coastal raids before they break inland routes.

levels.name: Aeetes' Boundary Stones
levels.historical_fact: Boundary stones symbolize royal authority in the traditions around Aeetes. Hold the frontier markers under rising pressure.

levels.name: Shrine of the Dragon
levels.historical_fact: The dragon returns in a stronger mythic rite near sacred ground. Survive this encounter to secure the chapter.

levels.name: Warrior Trial Grounds
levels.historical_fact: Late Colchian forces relied on disciplined local warriors and fortified movement routes. This trial map tests full tactical control.

levels.name: Rioni Marshlands
levels.historical_fact: Rioni marshlands could shield villages but punish poor positioning. Use terrain and pacing to survive converging waves.

levels.name: Ancient Palisade
levels.historical_fact: Palisade systems and gateworks became crucial as regional pressures intensified. Reinforce the line and deny breakthrough.

levels.name: Gate of the Golden Fleece
levels.historical_fact: The Golden Fleece gate is a ceremonial and strategic threshold in campaign lore. Hold the approach before the final stand.

levels.name: Heart of Colchis
levels.historical_fact: By 83 BC, Colchis fell under Pontic domination, but its people endured. Defend the Heart of Colchis in this mythic-historical finale.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 1
levels.historical_fact: King Pharnavaz I founded the Kingdom of Iberia around 299 BC, unifying Georgian tribes and establishing the first centralized Georgian state with administrative divisions called saeristavos.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 2
levels.historical_fact: The fortress of Armazi near Mtskheta housed the great idol of Armazi, the supreme sky and thunder god of pre-Christian Iberia, until Christianity replaced the old pagan religions.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 3
levels.historical_fact: Saint Nino of Cappadocia brought Christianity to Iberia in the early 4th century AD, making Georgia one of the earliest Christian kingdoms in the world — predating Rome's official adoption.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 4
levels.historical_fact: King Vakhtang Gorgasali established Tbilisi as the capital of Iberia. His wolf-head helmet gave him his Persian nickname 'Gorgasali,' meaning 'wolf-head.'

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 5
levels.historical_fact: The cave city of Uplistsikhe, one of the oldest known settlements in Georgia, was carved into rock and continuously inhabited from the early Iron Age through the medieval period.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 6
levels.historical_fact: Iberia's location between Rome and Persia made it a strategic battleground. Both empires vied for control of the Caucasian passes that commanded trade routes between Europe and Asia.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 7
levels.historical_fact: The pre-Christian Iberian pantheon included Armazi (sky/thunder), Zaden (fertility), Gatsi (moon), and Gaim (sun) — a syncretic system blending indigenous and Iranian influences.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 8
levels.historical_fact: Saint Nino's cross, made of vine branches bound with her own hair, remains one of Georgia's most sacred relics. The grapevine cross symbolizes the deep connection between Georgian Christianity and the land.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 9
levels.historical_fact: The ancient capital Mtskheta sits at the confluence of the Mtkvari and Aragvi rivers — one of the oldest continuously inhabited cities in the world and a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 10
levels.historical_fact: King Pharnavaz created the Georgian alphabet, according to medieval chronicles. The modern Georgian script (Mkhedruli) is one of only about 14 independently created writing systems in human history.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 11
levels.historical_fact: Iberian cavalry was renowned throughout the ancient Caucasus. Mounted archers and lance-bearing horsemen formed the core of the Iberian military, fighting both as Roman allies and independent forces.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 12
levels.historical_fact: The Sassanid Persian siege of Iberian fortresses tested the kingdom's stone defenses. Iberian engineers developed sophisticated wall construction techniques still visible at archaeological sites today.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 13
levels.historical_fact: Vakhtang Gorgasali died in battle against the Sassanid Persians in 522 AD. He is venerated as a saint in the Georgian Orthodox Church — a warrior-king who defied empires.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 14
levels.historical_fact: Roman legionaries periodically marched through Iberia during Rome's campaigns against Parthia. The Caucasus passes were vital for any army seeking to control the trade routes between empires.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 15
levels.historical_fact: The conversion of Iberia to Christianity was not merely religious but geopolitical — aligning Georgia with the Christian Roman world against Zoroastrian Persia, shaping centuries of Georgian history.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 16
levels.historical_fact: Iberian warriors adopted elements from both Roman and Persian military traditions, creating a unique fighting style that blended heavy infantry tactics with swift cavalry maneuvers.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 17
levels.historical_fact: The Sasanian war elephant terrified Iberian defenders unfamiliar with these massive beasts. Persian commanders used elephants as living siege engines against Caucasian fortifications.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 18
levels.historical_fact: The Iberian kingdom's wealth came from controlling mountain passes that carried silk road trade between the Black and Caspian Seas — a strategic position that brought both prosperity and invasion.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 19
levels.historical_fact: Parthian cavalry archers were among the most feared warriors of the ancient world. Their 'Parthian shot' — firing backwards while retreating — became a legendary military tactic.

levels.name: Kingdom of Iberia — Level 20
levels.historical_fact: The ancient city of Mtskheta's Svetitskhoveli Cathedral stands on the site where, according to tradition, Christ's robe is buried — making it one of Christianity's most sacred locations.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 1
levels.historical_fact: The Age of Invasions era (~630 – ~1089 AD) tested Georgian civilization to its limits.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 2
levels.historical_fact: Georgian defenders adapted their strategies to face new invaders during the Age of Invasions period.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 3
levels.historical_fact: The Age of Invasions era saw both devastating wars and remarkable cultural achievements in Georgian history.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 4
levels.historical_fact: Georgian fortifications evolved dramatically during the Age of Invasions period to counter new siege technologies.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 5
levels.historical_fact: The Age of Invasions era produced legendary heroes whose stories are still told in Georgian culture today.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 6
levels.historical_fact: Trade routes through Georgia during the Age of Invasions period brought both wealth and conflict.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 7
levels.historical_fact: Georgian military tactics during the Age of Invasions era were studied and admired by neighboring civilizations.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 8
levels.historical_fact: The mountainous terrain of the Caucasus provided natural defensive advantages during the Age of Invasions period.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 9
levels.historical_fact: Georgian religious and cultural identity persisted through the challenges of the Age of Invasions era.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 10
levels.historical_fact: The Age of Invasions era demonstrates the resilience and adaptability of Georgian civilization.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 11
levels.historical_fact: Georgian architecture from the Age of Invasions period still stands as testament to the skill of medieval builders.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 12
levels.historical_fact: Strategic mountain passes were crucial battlegrounds during the Age of Invasions era.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 13
levels.historical_fact: Georgian leaders during the Age of Invasions period navigated complex diplomatic relationships with neighboring empires.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 14
levels.historical_fact: The legacy of the Age of Invasions era continues to influence Georgian national identity today.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 15
levels.historical_fact: Georgian warriors of the Age of Invasions period developed unique fighting techniques adapted to the Caucasian terrain.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 16
levels.historical_fact: The Age of Invasions era saw significant developments in Georgian art, literature, and religious architecture.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 17
levels.historical_fact: Defensive tower construction reached new heights during the Age of Invasions period.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 18
levels.historical_fact: Georgian cavalry played a decisive role in battles throughout the Age of Invasions era.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 19
levels.historical_fact: The challenges of the Age of Invasions period forged a stronger sense of Georgian national identity.

levels.name: Age of Invasions — Level 20
levels.historical_fact: Historical records from the Age of Invasions era reveal a sophisticated and resilient Georgian society.
